ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for raita; closest parent is yogurt-based food product (FOODON_03302050).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for raita; closest parent is plain yoghurt (A00LC). Raita is a spiced, vegetable-mixed yogurt condiment.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for raita or a sufficiently close yogurt condiment concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raita as a side condiment is approximately 80 g, equivalent to a small bowl accompanying a main Indian meal.

nutrient_source.title: Dahi (Curd / Yogurt) with vegetables — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Raita is not listed as a standalone entry in ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain dahi (curd/yogurt) data combined with typical cucumber raita composition (approx. 70% plain low-fat yogurt, 20% cucumber, 10% spices/herbs). Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate values are weighted accordingly. Sodium reflects added salt typical of restaurant preparation. This is the closest defensible primary source for an Indian yogurt condiment served in Singapore.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for raita. GI is estimated from plain low-fat yogurt (GI ~36, University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Raita is predominantly yogurt (~70%) with low-GI vegetables (cucumber) and spices, which would not raise the GI above that of plain yogurt. GI of 36 is therefore a defensible upper-bound estimate. GL per serving = (36 × 5.8 × 0.80) / 100 = 1.67, rounded to 1.7.

hypertension.guidance: Raita contains approximately 210 mg sodium per 100 g due to added salt in preparation; individuals with hypertension should request low-salt versions or limit portion size to one small bowl (80 g) to keep sodium contribution below 170 mg per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 36 and a glycemic load of ~1.7 per 80 g serving, raita has a minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose and is a suitable condiment for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard side-dish portions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat and beneficial dairy nutrients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raita provides modest saturated fat (~1.7 g per 100 g) and contains calcium and potassium from yogurt, which are associated with cardiovascular benefit; however, sodium from added salt should be monitored in individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Raita is a traditional Indian yogurt-based condiment made by combining plain dahi (curd or yogurt) with finely chopped or grated vegetables — most commonly cucumber — along with roasted cumin, fresh coriander, mint, salt, and optionally green chilli. The mixture is chilled and served as a cooling side dish alongside spiced Indian meals such as biryani, pulao, kebabs, and roti. The texture is smooth and creamy with small vegetable pieces, and the flavour is mildly tangy, cooling, and aromatic from the spices. Raita plays a functional culinary role in balancing the heat and richness of heavily spiced dishes. In Singapore, raita is commonly served at Indian and South Asian restaurants, including banana leaf establishments, as a small side portion. Nutritionally, raita is a low-calorie, low-GI condiment providing approximately 62 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (~3.5 g) from yogurt, low fat (~2.8 g), and a small carbohydrate contribution (~5.8 g). It is a good source of calcium (~120 mg per 100 g) and riboflavin from dairy. The low glycemic index (~36) makes it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content (~210 mg per 100 g) from added salt is the primary clinical consideration for hypertensive individuals. Raita is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-compatible.
common_user_queries: Can I eat raita with biryani?
What is the difference between raita and tzatziki?
Is raita suitable for lactose intolerant people?

confidence_reason: Raita is not listed as a standalone entry in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain dahi data combined with typical cucumber raita composition. GI is estimated from plain yogurt GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium because the adaptation is methodologically sound but not based on a direct raita measurement.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain dahi (curd/yogurt) data weighted for typical cucumber raita composition (~70% yogurt, ~20% cucumber, ~10% spices/herbs). No direct raita entry exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a prepared dish. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for dahi. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as plain low-fat yogurt and cucumber contain no industrially produced trans fats; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central yogurt entries. 4. GI of 36 is an analogous estimate based on plain low-fat yogurt GI from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008). 5. The input record classified this food under 'Malaysian' cuisine and 'Grains and staples' food group; both have been corrected — raita is an Indian yogurt condiment, not Malaysian, and belongs to the Dairy and eggs food group. The restaurant serving it (Singapore Banana Leaf Restaurant) is an Indian restaurant, consistent with this correction. 6. Food group corrected from 'Grains and staples' to 'Dairy and eggs' based on primary ingredient (yogurt). Cuisine corrected from 'Malaysian' to 'Indian' based on dish origin and restaurant context. 7. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this dish is served in Singapore.
